definitions:
| TERM | Explanation | | Oracle ExaCC | Oracle Exadata Cloud@Customer. This is an engineered system optimized for running databases housed in the Cegeka Datacenters but managed as in the Oracle Cloud Infrastructure | | RDMA | RDMA stands for Remote Direct Memory Access and is an extension of the Direct Memory Access technology, which is the ability to access host memory directly without CPU intervention. RDMA allows for accessing memory data from one host to another. A key characteristic of RDMA is that it greatly improves throughput and performance while lowering latency because less CPU cycles are needed to process the network packets. | | OCI | Oracle Cloud Infrastructure (OCI) is a platform of cloud services that enables customers to build and run a wide range of applications in a highly available, consistently high-performance environment. | | RAC | Oracle Real Application Clusters (RAC) allow customers to run a single Oracle Database across multiple servers in order to maximize availability and enable horizontal scalability, while accessing shared storage. User sessions connecting to Oracle RAC instances can failover and safely replay changes during outages, without any changes to end user applications, hiding the impact of the outages from end users. | | MAA | Oracle Maximum Availability Architecture (MAA) provides architecture, configuration, and lifecycle best practices for Oracle Databases, enabling high-availability service levels for databases residing in on-premises, cloud, or hybrid configurations. | | ASM | Automatic Storage Management (ASM) is a purpose-built file system and volume manager for the Oracle database. ASM was first released with Oracle Database 10g. For Oracle databases, ASM simplified both the file system and volume management for the Oracle database. In addition to simplifying storage management, ASM improved file system scalability, performance, and database availability. These benefits hold for both single-instance databases as well as for Oracle Real Application Cluster (Oracle RAC) databases. |
TERM Explanation Oracle ExaCC Oracle Exadata Cloud@Customer. This is an engineered system optimized for running databases housed in the Cegeka Datacenters but managed as in the Oracle Cloud Infrastructure RDMA RDMA stands for Remote Direct Memory Access and is an extension of the Direct Memory Access technology, which is the ability to access host memory directly without CPU intervention. RDMA allows for accessing memory data from one host to another. A key characteristic of RDMA is that it greatly improves throughput and performance while lowering latency because less CPU cycles are needed to process the network packets. OCI Oracle Cloud Infrastructure (OCI) is a platform of cloud services that enables customers to build and run a wide range of applications in a highly available, consistently high-performance environment. RAC Oracle Real Application Clusters (RAC) allow customers to run a single Oracle Database across multiple servers in order to maximize availability and enable horizontal scalability, while accessing shared storage. User sessions connecting to Oracle RAC instances can failover and safely replay changes during outages, without any changes to end user applications, hiding the impact of the outages from end users. MAA Oracle Maximum Availability Architecture (MAA) provides architecture, configuration, and lifecycle best practices for Oracle Databases, enabling high-availability service levels for databases residing in on-premises, cloud, or hybrid configurations. ASM Automatic Storage Management (ASM) is a purpose-built file system and volume manager for the Oracle database. ASM was first released with Oracle Database 10g. For Oracle databases, ASM simplified both the file system and volume management for the Oracle database. In addition to simplifying storage management, ASM improved file system scalability, performance, and database availability. These benefits hold for both single-instance databases as well as for Oracle Real Application Cluster (Oracle RAC) databases.
service introduction: This document describes the Oracle Exadata Cloud@Customer (Oracle ExaCC) service to enable a high performing Oracle databases platform.
platform description: Oracle ExaCC is a hybrid cloud platform for Oracle databases. It is the database cloud platform for customers who desire cloud benefits but require their databases to be located on-premises. With Oracle ExaCC, customers can maintain absolute control over their data while leveraging the combined capabilities of Oracle Exadata and Oracle Cloud Infrastructure managed by Cegeka.
exadata hardware:
ExaCC is built with powerful database servers, scale-out intelligent storage servers, PMem, PCIe NVMe flash, and high-capacity disk drives. Internal connectivity between database and storage servers is enabled by a low-latency RoCE fabric. External connectivity to the ExaCC system is provided using standard 10 or 25 Gigabit Ethernet.
The database-optimized data tiering between DRAM, PMem, flash and disk implemented in Exadata provides lower latency, higher capacity, and faster performance than other flash- or PMem-based solutions. Flash and PMem storage arrays cannot match the throughput of Exadata's integrated and optimized architecture with full RoCE-based scale-out, high speed persistent memory, PCIe NVMe flash, offload of data intensive operations to storage, and algorithms that are specifically optimized for databases.
exadata software:
The technology that enables Exadata’s unparalleled performance without any of the bottlenecks of traditional storage arrays is Exadata Storage Server software. This software powers the Exadata storage servers, providing an extremely efficient and database-optimized storage infrastructure. All Exadata Storage Server software features are included in ExaCC.
One of the many unique features of Exadata Storage Server software is Smart Scan technology, which offloads data intensive SQL operations from the database servers directly into the storage servers. By pushing SQL processing to the storage servers, data filtering and processing occur immediately and in parallel across all storage servers, as data is read from disk and flash. Only the rows and columns that are directly relevant to a query are sent to the database servers. This greatly accelerates analytic queries, eliminates bottlenecks, and significantly reduces the CPU usage of the database servers.
In addition to Smart Scan, Exadata includes a vast array of software capabilities that enables its unparalleled scalability, performance and availability. Some of these Exadata software features are:
The above described hardware and software features result in a set of platform metrics as described in the data sheet issued by Oracle for an Exadata Cloud@Customer Quarter Rack:
(https://www.oracle.com/a/ocom/docs/engineered-systems/exadata/exadb-cc-x10m-ds.pdf)
The datasheet indicates maximum values, but real-life performance depends mainly on the customer application architecture.
availability:
Availability of the ExaCC platform is measured by time slot during a 365/24/7 window. The combined setup of VM clusters on ExaCC platforms in primary and remote datacenter has an SLA of 99.9% The platform is highly redundant by design and should overcome to a large extent hardware failures on server, network, storage and power supply components. However, Oracle does not guarantee any Service Level Agreements (SLA), but rather Service Level Objectives (SLO) on the hardware and platform availability. This means that for critical hardware failure and its replacement they will do everything within reasonable effort to resolve the service with the shortest delay. Consequently, the SLA of 99.9% can only be guaranteed with a total solution as highlighted in red on the diagram below for the solution architecture. This means a maximum service outage of the combined cluster service of 43’28” per month. In case no remote VM cluster is foreseen at the remote datacenter, the SLA drops to 99,7% (outage of 2hr 10’ 24” per month) due to the dependencies on external factors where Cegeka has no impact upon. Although the OS and Database management services are not part of this service it is important to note the following considerations in terms of the entire solution architecture. Oracle Maximum Availability Architecture (MAA) provides architecture, configuration, and lifecycle best practices for Oracle Databases, enabling high-availability service levels for databases. To reach the maximum potential of the ExaCC platform, Cegeka recommends aligning the entire solution architecture (ExaCC clusters + database infrastructure) to the Oracle Maximum Availability Architecture as published by Oracle: (https://www.oracle.com/database/technologies/maximum-availability-architecture) In practice this means having a two node VM-cluster in primary and remote data center, with a primary RAC database in one location and a standby RAC database in the other. Database infrastructure highlighted in orange on the diagram below. By combining all possibilities in a smart way, the DBA can optimally guarantee the database service availability. While customers will opt in most cases for the MAA as indicated above, some might consider a solution with a database in balanced RAC. This possibility enables to economize on capacity allocation (#SKU’s). In practice this means that one of both instances is not online during normal operations. Only is case of anomaly this second instance can be brought online for as long as enough CPU and memory is available on the surviving VM. Alternatively, the customer should prioritize on the business importance of the different databases in a shutdown plan. With this choice the customer doesn’t benefit anymore from the redundancy built into the VM Cluster and consequently the SLA drops to 99.7. In the event the customer does not implement RAC at all, SLAs (service level agreements) drop significantly. This is not a recommended setup and as such the customer accepts implicitly the consequences described above in paragraph 1.5.2. Images of the different concepts on how database solutions can be implemented on the platform are attached in appendix A. Combining both the limitation on having no remote cluster and not the Full RAC setup SLA drops towards lower SLA’s as indicated in below table. | | Platform level | Database level | | | VM Cluster | Full RAC | Balanced RAC | No RAC (not recommended) | | Only Primary cluster in 1 Datacenter | 99.7% | 99.7% | 99.5% | 98.0% | | Primary + Remote cluster spread over 2 Datacenters | 99.9% | 99.9% | 99.7% | 99.5% |
Platform level Database level VM Cluster Full RAC Balanced RAC No RAC (not recommended) Only Primary cluster in 1 Datacenter 99.7% 99.7% 99.5% 98.0% Primary + Remote cluster spread over 2 Datacenters 99.9% 99.9% 99.7% 99.5% Since the ExaCC VM cluster on itself doesn’t deliver real business data, the Recovery Point Objective (RPO) and Recovery Time Objective (RTO) are not determined on a cluster level but rather on a database level and are therefore not part of this service. This also applies to Service Level Agreements for Disaster Recovery operations.
multiple virtual machine clusters: Database services run securely in Virtual Machine (VM) Clusters running on the ExaCC infrastructure. Each ExaCC can run multiple VM Clusters to increase value through greater consolidation. They provide isolated operating environments for different database workloads through separate access rules and network configurations as well as customizable compute, memory, and storage resources. Each VM Cluster can run Autonomous Database or Exadata Database Service, which allows Autonomous Database to be deployed alongside Exadata Database Service on the same infrastructure, eliminating the need to deploy separate systems for these services.
shared service offered: Cegeka offers the ExaCC platform in a multi-customer architecture to run database services and uses the Oracle Cloud Infrastructure Measure for Computing (OCPU) as the unit of measure to allocate and charge capacity per customer. The OCPU measure is used both for hardware allocation as well as for consumption. Hardware allocation is done through several capacity resources (processor, memory and storage) reserved for each customer based on the predicted usage per cluster during a period of time. This is a more static component which requires strategic insight on long term commitment of the customer. Next to the hardware allocation there is also a consumption part. OCI provides a way of measuring the actual consumption of all the resource types used in the ExaCC platform. This consumption is measured on an OCPU per hour basis. For this item operational insight in the customer way of working and variation in workload are important factors to know.
unit of measure: The unit of measure to allocate capacity per customer uses OCPU. For both the Exadata Platform Capacity and Support Services and the Exadata Platform Consumption this translates in the following:
co terminus or yearly reservation:
An ExaCC customer with Cegeka has two contract options. The longer-term commitment of the customer and Cegeka is defined as a Co Terminus contract while a shorter-term commitment is defined as a Yearly Reservation.
Co Terminus – meaning the customer commits to pay the hardware allocation from start of contract until the end of life (EOL) of this platform. EOL is a fixed time when the platform will be decommissioned. It is upfront known and can’t be prolonged. The total duration of the contract for the customer depends on the moment he steps onto the current platform. This lowers the price for his part of the platform capacity. In the event the customer leaves Cegeka earlier than the end date of his commitment, then he will still pay the amount of unbilled hardware fees for the remaining period before his exit from Cegeka.
Yearly Reservation – meaning the customer commits for 12 months hardware reservation on the platform. In the event the customer leaves Cegeka earlier than the end date of his commitment, then he will still pay the amount of unbilled hardware fees for the remaining period before his exit from Cegeka. No shorter periods than 12 months are allowed. This fee per unit is higher than the Co Terminus because of the higher risk for Cegeka to have underused systems during longer periods.

transition services: Transition services are not offered as part of the ExaCC platform services. For both transition phases – Transition In for onboarding and Transition Out for offboarding – a separate project plan will need to be developed and costed.
transition in:
A customer should comply with the following minimum requirements to be able to onboard on the ExaCC platform:
Bringing the customer to the minimum requirements should then be part of the Transition In project either before or during the onboarding process.
It is highly recommended to consider the following features to increase the database service availability (depending on the required service level of your database they might become required; please check the Oracle database management service description for more details):
transition out:
When approaching the end of the committed hardware reservation period, the customer should ultimately 3 months prior to the end date of this commitment inform Cegeka about its intentions to either extend the contract or not. It is up to Cegeka to decide whether there is the capacity to extend the current installation, to advise moving to another platform or stop the current solution.
At all times, when the committed period ends, the systems will be terminated on the end date, while it remains the customer's responsibility to recuperate its assets and data prior to that end date.
In the event the customer leaves Cegeka earlier than the end date of his commitment, then he will still pay the amount of unbilled hardware fees for the remaining period before his exit from Cegeka.
run services:
The run service for ExaCC consists of two components as described in chapter 1.2.2 Shared Service Offered. The first component – Exadata Platform Capacity and Support Services – covers the hardware reservation and all supporting services from Cegeka and Oracle to maintain the Exadata hardware and software. The second component is needed when installing databases on the platform and is called the Exadata Platform Consumption.
Note that the management of the OS (Operating System) and databases is covered in a separate managed service offering and is not part of this service.
exadata platform capacity and support se:
ExaCC is an engineered system with integrated storage, memory and CPU that needs to be allocated to a dedicated database cluster of two nodes. One unit or SKU consists of:
A minimum consumption unit for these resources has been defined. The minimum cluster configuration starts at 5 SKUs with an increment possible per 1 SKU. The maximum number of SKUs per cluster is 62. To calculate the number of total SKUs needed you should determine which resource (processor, memory, storage) has the highest usage per cluster based on the following algorithm:
roundup(max((#OPCU/2), (#GB RAM/44), (#TB disk/3)))
Every VM in a cluster has a local storage allocated of 184 GB for OS binaries (/u01) and an additional 96 GB for Oracle Home installations and other binaries (/u02).
exadata platform consumption: ExaCC provides a way of measuring the actual consumption of all the resource types (compute, network, storage) used on the ExaCC platform. This consumption is measured on an OCPU per hour basis. Once you deploy Oracle databases on the ExaCC platform you will also need to pay for the platform consumption using a subscription model. Cegeka currently supports two software licensing subscription models:
bring your own license (byol):
In this scenario a customer needs a minimal Oracle Database Enterprise Edition license. Oracle Database Standard Edition is not supported on ExaCC. When a customer brings an Oracle Database Enterprise Edition license entitlement to ExaCC, they are granted the rights to use Transparent Data Encryption (TDE), Diagnostics Pack, Tuning Pack, Data Masking and Subsetting Pack, and Real Application Testing without bringing license entitlements for those Database Options and Management Packs. The standard unit price will be at a much lower rate than the one in the next scenario.
Although Real Application Cluster (RAC) is not an absolute requirement for databases on ExaCC, Cegeka highly recommends customers to make use of this feature to augment the database service availability.
Not applying RAC means that the customer accepts the following constraints:
license included (li):
This subscription model includes all the features of Oracle Database Enterprise Edition, plus all the Oracle Database Enterprise Manager Packs and all Oracle Database Enterprise Edition Options. These industry-leading capabilities include Database In-Memory, Real Application Clusters (Oracle RAC), Multitenant, Active Data Guard, Automatic Storage Management (ASM), Partitioning, Advanced Compression, Advanced Security, Database Vault, Real Application Testing, OLAP, Advanced Analytics and Spatial and Graph.
This subscription model is ideal for customers without existing Oracle Database licenses, or customers seeking to use Oracle Database features beyond what they are currently licensed to use. Also, as you pay based on a consumption model, this formula can be advantageous for non production environments with limited service windows.
The standard unit price is higher than the one in the BYOL scenario because of the licenses being part of the consumption model.
TIP: When moving to this model, customers can place their Oracle current licenses on hold, preventing to pay support fees any longer.
universal credits (uc) versus pay as you:
Once a customer has established its software licensing subscription model (BYOL or LI) they can opt for a prepaid universal credit (UC) or a pay-as-you-go (PAYG) payment model for the OCPU per hour charges. In the PAYG model customers will be charged a flat fee without discounts for their actual consumption. The customer will receive a monthly invoice with an overview of the consumed OCPUs per hour multiplied by their unit price. This unit price will be different depending on the software licensing subscription model chosen (BYOL or LI). In the UC model customers together with Cegeka will calculate their annual predicted consumption and commit to a guaranteed annual consumption. This upfront commitment from the customer is rewarded by means of discounts applied per block of prepaid amount (see table below). This annual prepaid cloud consumption is valid for 12 months and is not transferable to the next period. When customers have consumed their prepaid consumption, they will be charged for the overage consumptions on a monthly basis for the remaining period at the same price agreed at the beginning of the period. | Consumption Approach | Burn rate on prepaid UC | Price for overage | PAYG | | NO UC | | | 100% | | UC < 100 k€ | 100% | 100% | 100% | | UC from 100k€ - 500 k€ | 95% | 95% | 100% | | UC from 500k€ - 1m€ | 90% | 90% | 100% | | UC from 1m€ - 5m€ | 85% | 85% | 100% |
Consumption Approach Burn rate on prepaid UC Price for overage PAYG NO UC 100% UC < 100 k€ 100% 100% 100% UC from 100k€ - 500 k€ 95% 95% 100% UC from 500k€ - 1m€ 90% 90% 100% UC from 1m€ - 5m€ 85% 85% 100% Hints: Dynamic OCPU allocation Customers have a fixed number of SKUs allocated for their Exadata Platform Capacity per cluster for the total period of subscription. However, in terms of cost efficiency, they can to a large extent influence the Exadata Platform Consumption by dynamically allocating the required number of OCPUs at any given time. Most customers have a variable workload where it is advised to deallocate OCPUs during low workload periods and burst their number of OCPUs during high workload periods. This can easily be achieved based on a schedule or upon request. The number of OCPUs on a running cluster is always minimum 4 OCPU and maximum the number of OCPUs contained within the SKUs per cluster in the Exadata Platform Capacity. If an environment is not running, then no consumption is accounted for. Typical examples where customers can economize on their Exadata Cloud Consumption:
standard changes:
Standard changes which are implemented in the Order Portal are called Order Portal Changes. As the addition or retirement of standard changes is dynamic, please refer to the Order Portal for a complete and current overview of available standard changes. Standard changes which are not yet implemented in the Order Portal, can be requested to be added using a formalized request and approval process.
Non-standard changes are billed in time and material.
platform patching and maintenance interv:
By default, rolling patching will be used to achieve zero downtime during platform patching. Depending on the database options used (e.g., No RAC or Balanced RAC), non-rolling patching can be mandatory because of the customer’s choice. In practice customers should consider planned interruptions of their database service for a short duration in the patching events. (See constraints mentioned in 1.5.2)
Cegeka will determine the most optimal or least impacting time window for common interventions on the platform based on the overall customers most idle workloads. By signing up for this service the customer accepts the limitation of being part of a shared platform where activity of other customers is spread in time. Cegeka has the final word in this decision.
The type of intervention can be: the platform patching, platform maintenance, security threat mitigation, hardware defect urgency or any action related to the good (pro-active) management of the system.
Cegeka will inform customers prior to the interventions through the existing change management processes or in case of unexpected outage through the incident management processes.
priority levels:
Incidents related to the managed ExaCC platform will be dealt with pro-actively as much as possible. In case the customer is raising incidents the following priority levels apply: | Priority | Examples | | 1 – Critical | ExaCC platform unavailable for multiple customers ExaCC cluster unavailable for all end-users of one customer | | 2 – High | ExaCC cluster unavailable for multiple (not all) end-users of one customer One Virtual Machine of an ExaCC cluster not available for end-users | | 3 – Medium | | | 4 - Low | |
Priority Examples 1 – Critical 2 – High 3 – Medium 4 - Low Incidents can only be logged with their priority level for as long as they are applicable to the service window determined upfront by the customer.

Oracle (Vendor) Cegeka CUSTOMER Exadata Platform Capacity and Support Services RAC I Exadata Platform Consumption registration R A CI Patch ExaCC platform RA C I Patch VM cluster, GI and DB RA CI Responsible: The party who is responsible for the execution of the activity. Accountable: The party who delegates the execution of the activity and is the last one to review the task or deliverable before it’s deemed complete. Consulted: The party who must be consulted and must approve or provide input prior to execution. This party (partly) gives direction to the result. This is about two-way communication. Informed: That is the party who is informed about the decisions, progress and results achieved, so that the next step can be taken. This is about one-way communication.
